note_data.title: Equilibrium
keyPoints: Equilibrium is the state of a body in which there is no change in its motion
First condition of equilibrium: The sum of all forces acting on a body must be zero (ΣF = 0)
Second condition of equilibrium: The sum of all torques acting on a body about any point must be zero (Στ = 0)
Stable equilibrium: When a body returns to its original position after being slightly displaced
Unstable equilibrium: When a body moves further away from its original position after being slightly displaced
Neutral equilibrium: When a body remains in equilibrium in its new position after being displaced
Centre of gravity is the point where the whole weight of the body appears to act
For a body to be in complete equilibrium, both translational and rotational equilibrium conditions must be satisfied

definitions.definition: Equilibrium is the state of a body in which there is no change in its motion.
A body is said to be in stable equilibrium if it returns to its original position when slightly displaced.
A body is said to be in unstable equilibrium if it moves further away from its original position when slightly displaced.
A body is said to be in neutral equilibrium if it remains in equilibrium in its new position when displaced.
Centre of gravity of a body is the point where the whole weight of the body appears to act.

importantQuestions.question: Define stable, unstable and neutral equilibrium with examples.
importantQuestions.answer: Stable equilibrium: Body returns to original position when displaced (e.g., pendulum at rest). Unstable equilibrium: Body moves away when displaced (e.g., pencil on its tip). Neutral equilibrium: Body remains in new position when displaced (e.g., ball on flat surface).

importantQuestions.question: State and explain the conditions of equilibrium. Derive the mathematical expressions for both conditions.
importantQuestions.answer: First condition: For translational equilibrium, sum of all forces must be zero. ΣF = 0 or ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0. This means net force in any direction is zero. Second condition: For rotational equilibrium, sum of all torques about any point must be zero. Στ = 0. This ensures no rotational motion. For complete equilibrium, both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously.

mcqs.question: A body is in stable equilibrium when:

options.B: It returns to original position when displaced
options.C: It remains in new position when displaced

commonMistakes: Students confuse stable and unstable equilibrium definitions
Forgetting to write both conditions of equilibrium in long answers
Not drawing proper diagrams for types of equilibrium
Mixing up centre of gravity with centre of mass concepts
Not stating the mathematical expressions ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0 clearly
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question about types of equilibrium in BSEK papers
Always draw diagrams for stable, unstable and neutral equilibrium - appears every year
Memorize exact definitions word by word from STBB textbook
Long questions often ask to derive both conditions of equilibrium together
Centre of gravity questions frequently appear in combination with equilibrium
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw three diagrams showing: 1) Cone on broad base (stable), 2) Cone on pointed tip (unstable), 3) Ball/cylinder on flat surface (neutral). Also draw a balanced see-saw showing forces and distances for moment calculations.
pastPaperRefs: BSEK 2023 SSC-II Q3 (4 marks) — asked students to define equilibrium and explain the conditions required for a body to be in equilibrium
BSEK 2022 SSC-II Q7 (6 marks) — required differentiation between stable, unstable and neutral equilibrium with examples
BSEK 2024 SSC-II Q4 (3 marks) — tested definition of center of gravity and its relationship with equilibrium
BSEK 2021 SSC-II Q6 (5 marks) — asked to explain types of equilibrium with real-life examples and diagrams

workedExample.question: Define equilibrium. Explain the three types of equilibrium with examples. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Write the textbook definition of equilibrium
Step 2: Define stable equilibrium with example
Step 3: Define unstable equilibrium with example
Step 4: Define neutral equilibrium with example
workedExample.answer: Equilibrium is the state of a body in which it remains at rest or continues to move with uniform velocity when no net external force acts upon it. Stable equilibrium: When a body is displaced slightly from its equilibrium position, it returns to its original position, e.g., a ball at the bottom of a bowl. Unstable equilibrium: When a body is displaced slightly, it moves further away from its equilibrium position, e.g., a pencil balanced on its tip. Neutral equilibrium: When displaced, the body remains in equilibrium at its new position, e.g., a ball on a flat surface.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for equilibrium definition, 1 mark each for defining the three types, 1 mark each for correct examples

examinerWarnings: Students often lose marks by writing general answers instead of textbook-exact definitions. Always use the STBB textbook phrasing for equilibrium definitions.
Many students confuse stable and unstable equilibrium examples. Remember: stable returns to original position, unstable moves away from it.
Avoid using complex physics terminology beyond the syllabus. Sindh Board expects simple, clear textbook language, not advanced concepts like torque or moment of inertia.
note_data.quickRecap: Equilibrium occurs when no net force acts on a body, keeping it at rest or in uniform motion. Three types exist: stable (returns when displaced), unstable (moves away when displaced), and neutral (stays in new position). Examples include ball in bowl (stable), pencil on tip (unstable), and ball on flat surface (neutral). Always use exact textbook definitions.
importantFormulas: Sum of upward forces = Sum of downward forces
